description: The provided image displays a publisher 'Check for updates' icon with a bookmark symbol inside a circular border; no scientific data, curves, or micrographs are visually present, and the caption is empty. The accompanying text extraction summary reports that the study investigates a novel Fe-Mn-Al-C-Cr duplex lightweight steel (nominal composition 30.35Mn-12.60Al-1.32C-4.83Cr wt.%) exhibiting an ultralow density of 6.36 g·cm⁻³. The research examines microstructural evolution and tensile properties across multiple annealing conditions at 950°C, 1000°C, and 1050°C for durations ranging from 1 to 5 min. Key findings include the formation of austenite-ferrite duplex microstructures, intragranular κ-carbide nanoparticles in the DP1000-5 condition, and a specific ultimate tensile strength-total elongation product surpassing 8 GPa·g⁻¹·cm³·%. Because these material details are not directly visible in the image pixels, they rely entirely on the extracted text summary rather than visual observation.

Figure 1 presents the phase evolution and microstructural characterization of a cold-rolled (CR) Fe-Mn-Al-C-Cr duplex lightweight steel after annealing at 600–1050 °C for 5 min. Panel (a) shows X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns revealing that the CR state consists of austenite (FCC, red diamonds) and ferrite (BCC, blue diamonds). As annealing temperature increases, a β phase (brown diamonds) emerges at 700–900 °C, followed by Cr₇C₃ carbides (orange diamonds) and κ-carbides (green diamonds) at 950–1000 °C; these carbides dissolve significantly above 1000 °C, yielding a duplex FCC+BCC structure at 1050 °C. Panel (b) presents TEM bright-field images, EDS mapping, and selected-area electron diffraction (SAED, B=[0001]) of sample DP950–5, identifying Cr₇C₃ carbides precipitated at grain boundaries with sizes of hundreds of nanometers. Panel (c) shows TEM micrographs and SAED patterns of DP1000–5, distinguishing ferrite (zone 2, B=[023]) from austenite containing intragranular κ-carbide nanoparticles (zone 3, B=[011]); high-resolution TEM quantifies these nanoparticles as 0.86 ± 0.20 nm in radius with a volume fraction of 1.56 %. Collectively, the data demonstrate that annealing at 1000 °C for 5 min produces a fully recrystallized austenite-ferrite duplex matrix strengthened by ultrafine intragranular κ-carbides rather than brittle intergranular carbides, underpinning the observed strength-ductility synergy.

The figure presents EBSD phase (PH) mappings (a1–f1) and grain-reference-orientation-deviation (GROD) mappings of austenite (a2–f2) for a Fe-Mn-Al-C-Cr duplex low-density steel under different processing conditions: cold-rolled (CR), and annealed at 950°C, 1000°C, or 1050°C for 1 or 5 min (DP950-1, DP950-5, DP1000-1, DP1000-5, DP1050-1). The PH maps distinguish FCC austenite (cyan) from BCC ferrite (red), while GROD maps color-code low-deviation recrystallized grains (yellow, 0°–2°) against deformed regions (pink to 180°), with high-angle grain boundaries (>15°) marked as dark lines. The CR sample exhibits deformed austenite containing shear bands. Annealing at 950°C produces the highest ferrite fraction with the thickest δ-ferrite bands and coarsest granular α-ferrite, accompanied by only partial recrystallization. As temperature increases to 1000–1050°C, ferrite content decreases markedly, austenite becomes fully recrystallized, annealing twins emerge, and austenite grain size grows from 5.13 ± 4.74 μm to 9.65 ± 8.17 μm. These observations demonstrate that elevated annealing temperatures promote ferrite-to-austenite transformation, complete recrystallization, and grain coarsening, which govern the subsequent strength-ductility synergy.

This figure presents the mechanical characterization of a novel duplex low-density steel under varying annealing conditions through two complementary panels. Panel (a) displays engineering stress–strain curves for specimens annealed at 950 °C (DP950-1, DP950-5), 1000 °C (DP1000-1, DP1000-5, DP1000-5-TD), and 1050 °C (DP1050-1), where higher annealing temperatures and longer times generally reduce yield strength while enhancing ductility; notably, DP1000-5 and DP1050-1 exhibit superior strength–ductility synergy. Panel (b) maps specific ultimate tensile strength (SUTS) against total elongation (TE) via an Ashby-style chart, comparing the developed steels (red stars) against 2000-series Al alloys, Ti6Al4V, austenitic steels, and duplex steels. Contour lines denote the SUTS×TE product from 2 to 8 GPa·g⁻¹·cm³·%. The developed DP1000-5 and DP1050-1 surpass the 8 GPa·g⁻¹·cm³·% threshold, outperforming existing high-strength metallic materials, while DP1000-5-TD shows reduced TE due to transverse loading relative to δ-ferrite bands. Collectively, the figure substantiates that the designed duplex steel achieves an unprecedented specific strength–ductility combination.

This figure presents paired SEM (left column) and EBSD phase maps (right column) revealing crack-path morphology and phase distribution in duplex low-density steel specimens annealed at 1000°C for 5 min. Panels (a1) and (a2) correspond to DP1000–5 (tensile-tested along the rolling direction, RD), while (b1) and (b2) correspond to DP1000–5-TD (tested along the transverse direction, TD). In DP1000–5, the SEM image (a1) shows crack nucleation at the α/γ interface, propagation along the normal direction (ND), and marked crack deflection upon encountering δ-ferrite bands aligned parallel to the RD; the accompanying EBSD map (a2) confirms these BCC (red) ferrite bands within the FCC (cyan) austenite matrix and their interaction with the crack. By contrast, DP1000–5-TD (b1, b2) exhibits cracks initiating at both α/γ and δ/γ interfaces where the δ-ferrite bands are perpendicular to the tensile axis; the cracks propagate along these bands and quickly connect across the specimen thickness. The comparison demonstrates that δ-ferrite bands parallel to the tensile direction promote crack deflection and blunting, enhancing ductility, whereas perpendicular bands provide easy through-thickness crack linkage, explaining the lower total elongation measured in the TD-oriented sample.
